title: Search Algorithms
overview: Search is foundational in AI — finding a path from start to goal. Learn BFS, DFS, Greedy Best-First, and A* search.

blocks.title: What is a Search Problem?
blocks.body: A **search problem** involves an agent moving from an **initial state** to a **goal state** by taking **actions** through a **state space** (all possible configurations).
bullets: Goal state — where you want to arrive
Actions — valid moves from each state
Path cost — how expensive a solution is

blocks.title: Think of Navigating a Maze
blocks.text: Your position is your state. Walls limit your actions (movements). Finding a path from entrance to exit is the search problem.

centerPanel.label: Search Components
centerPanel.textContent:
Initial State = Starting point
Actions = Valid moves
Goal State = Destination
Path Cost = Distance or time

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
# Search problem example
start = 'Home'
goal = 'School'
actions = ['North', 'South', 'East', 'West']
print(f'Find path: {start} → {goal}')

blocks.question: In a maze, what is the goal state called?
blocks.answer: The goal state is the destination — the configuration you want to reach. In a maze, it's typically the exit cell marked G.

blocks.title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
blocks.body: **BFS** explores all neighbors at the current level before going deeper — like ripples spreading in water. It uses a queue (FIFO) and **guarantees the shortest path**.
bullets: Explores level by level — all nearby options first
Uses a queue: first discovered, first explored
Guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs

blocks.title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
blocks.body: **DFS** goes deep into one path before backtracking — like following one corridor to the end. It uses a stack (LIFO) and uses **less memory** but may not find the shortest path.
bullets: Explores one branch fully before trying others
Uses a stack: last discovered, first explored

blocks.title: BFS = Checking Every Shelf on Floor 1 First
blocks.text: Before going to Floor 2, BFS checks every store on Floor 1. DFS is like going deep into one hallway, exploring all side rooms before backtracking.

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Floor-by-Floor vs Deep Exploration
leftPanel.description: BFS: systematic levels. DFS: deep then backtrack.

centerPanel.label: Algorithm Comparison
centerPanel.textContent:
BFS:
• Queue (FIFO)
• Shortest path ✓
• More memory

DFS:
• Stack (LIFO)
• Less memory
• No shortest guarantee

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
from collections import deque
# BFS: queue.popleft()
# DFS: stack.pop()
rightPanel.output:
BFS: level by level
DFS: deep first

blocks.question: Which search guarantees the shortest path?
blocks.answer: BFS guarantees the shortest path because it explores all nodes at distance 1, then distance 2, and so on. The first path to the goal is always the shortest.

blocks.title: Greedy Best-First & A* Search
blocks.body: **Greedy Best-First** uses a heuristic (estimate) to pick the most promising next step. **A*** combines actual cost so far g(n) AND estimated cost h(n) — making it both optimal and efficient.
bullets: Heuristic h(n) — estimated cost from current node to goal
Greedy uses only h(n) — can be fast but not optimal
A* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n) — cost so far + estimate
A* is used in GPS navigation and game AI

blocks.title: A* = GPS That Considers Distance Traveled AND Estimated Distance
blocks.text: GPS doesn't explore blindly. It considers how far you've driven AND estimates remaining distance — just like A* combines g(n) and h(n).

leftPanel.label: Step 1
leftPanel.title: Start at Initial State
leftPanel.description: Begin with g(n)=0, estimate h(n) to goal

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
# A* formula
f_n = g_n + h_n
# g_n = cost so far
# h_n = estimate to goal
print(f'f(n) = {f_n}')

blocks.question: What does A* use that Greedy Best-First does not?
blocks.answer: A* uses BOTH cost so far (g(n)) AND estimated cost to goal (h(n)). Greedy only uses the heuristic h(n), ignoring how far you've already traveled.

blocks.title: Explore a Simple Graph
blocks.instructions: Create a dictionary representing a graph with at least 4 nodes. Print all neighbors of a given node.
blocks.hint: Use graph[node] to access the list of neighbors for any node.

keyTakeaways.text: A search problem has a start state, actions, and a goal state
BFS finds the shortest path by exploring level by level
DFS goes deep first — uses less memory but may not find shortest path
Greedy search uses a heuristic to pick the best next step
A* combines cost so far + estimated cost = most efficient search
Search is used in maps, games, puzzles, and route planning

whatYouLearned: How to model a problem as a search problem
The difference between BFS and DFS
When to choose which search algorithm
How to represent graphs in Python
